Oracc: CAMS/GKAB — CAMS Geography of Knowledge Corpus

TCL 06, 41 [Nocturnal rituals][via cams/gkab]

Obverse
oo NaN  (start of obverse missing)
o 1'o 1'

ina muḫ-ḫi x x [x] x pa-ni gišNIG₂.GIDRU i-dag-gal ki-⸢ma⸣ x [...]

(o 1') [...] waits for the sceptre on the ... [...] .... As ... [...], the sceptre and the shoe (for dressing the god) arise, and the gods and goddesses go in front of him and behind him, as before. They go down to the main court and take up position (facing) towards Anu.

o 2'2'

gišNIG₂.GIDRU u₃ še-e-nu ZI-MEŠ-nim-ma DINGIR-MEŠ u₃ dINANA-MEŠ ki-ma maḫ-⸢ri⸣-[im-ma]

o 3'3'

ina pa-ni-šu₂ u₃ EGIR-šu₂ DUak a-na KISAL.MAḪ E₁₁-MEŠ-ma a-na d60 LA₂⸢aṣ⸣

o 4'4'

lu₂MAŠ.MAŠ gišNIG₂.GIDRU u₂-ḫabab-ma KU₄-ma ina šub-ti-šu₂ TUŠab dPAP.SUKKAL dNUSKA

(o 4') A mašmaššu-priest purifies the sceptre, and the sceptre goes in and sits on his seat. Papsukkal, Nusku and Usmu sit on the seats in the forecourt of Anu, and the shoe, the Marat-Anu and the Marat-Uruk come back and the shoe goes into the Enir, the temple with the gold bed of Antu, and is placed on a footstool. The Marat-Anu and the Marat-Uruk sit on the seats in the forecourt of Antu. He mixes wine and good oil together and at the gate of the cella libates to Anu, Antu and all the gods.

o 5'5'

u₃ dARA ina KISAL d60 ina muḫ-ḫi šu-bat-MEŠ TUŠab u₃ še-e-nu dDUMU.MUNUS-MEŠ—d⸢60⸣

o 6'6'

u₃ dDUMU.MUNUS-MEŠ—UNUGki GURru-MEŠ-nim-ma še-e-nu a-na E₂.NIR E₂ gišNU₂ KU₃.⸢SI₂₂⸣

o 7'7'

ša₂ an-tu₄ KU₄-ma ina muḫ-ḫi GIR₃.GUB.BU iš-šak-kan dDUMU.MUNUS-MEŠ—d⸢60⸣

o 8'8'

u₃ dDUMU.MUNUS-MEŠ—UNUGki ina KISAL an-tu₄ ina muḫ-ḫi šu-bat-MEŠ TUŠab GEŠTIN u₃ I₃.GIŠ DU₁₀.GA

o 9'9'

iš-te-niš ḪI.ḪI-ma ina KA₂ e₂pa-pa-ḫa a-na d60 an-tu₄ u₃ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI i-naq-qa

o 10'10'

sip-pi-MEŠ ša₂ KA₂ e₂pa-pa-ḫa gišIG-MEŠ u₃ KA₂-MEŠ u₂-lap-pat NIG₂.NAqa-MEŠ KU₃.SI₂₂

(o 10') He sprinkles the door-jambs of the gate of the cella, the doors and the gates. He fills the gold censers and presents an offering, an ox and a ram, to Anu, Antu and all the gods.

o 11'11'

u₂-mal₂-le-e-ma SISKUR₂ GU₄ u₃ UDU.NITA₂ a-na d60 an-tu₄ u₃ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI BAL[qi₂]

o 12'12'

KIN.SIG ša₂ li-lat a-na d60 an-tu₄ u₃ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI i-qar-rub

(o 12') The meal of the evening is presented to Anu, Antu and all the gods.

o 13'13'

⸢ul⸣ DU₈ar₂ ba-a-a-at i-ba-at KA₂ ul ut-ta-dal a-na DINGIR-MEŠ ma-la ina KISAL

(o 13') It is not cleared away. The nocturnal offering stays overnight. The gate is not shut. The meal is presented to as many gods as are sitting in the forecourt. During the evening watch, on the roof of the Baramah, the ziggurat of the Reš, as soon as great Anu of heaven rises, (and) great Antu of heaven rises in the Wagon, 'To the image of beautified appearance, the star of the heavens' (and) 'Anu, the king, has come forth, the beautiful constellation' (are recited). You set up a gold offering table for Anu and Antu of heaven. You raise the water for the hands towards Anu and Antu of heaven and set out the offering table. You set out meat from an ox, meat from a ram, and birds. You set up finest beer together with pressed wine. You make all the orchard fruit splendid (as offerings).

o 14'14'

[aš?]-⸢ba?⸣ nap-tan i-qar-rub ina EN.NUN.USAN ina u₂-ru BARA₂.MAḪ ziq-qur-⸢rat⸣

o 15'15'

ša₂ e₂re-eš ki-ma ša₂ muld60 GALu₂ ša₂ ANe it-tap-ḫa an-tu₄ GALtu₄ ša₂ ANe

o 16'16'

ina mulMAR.GID₂.DA it-tap-ḫa a-na tam-šil zi-i-mu bu-un-ne₂-e MUL ša₂-ma-mi

o 17'17'

da-nu₃ LUGAL it-ta-ṣa-a ṣa-lam ba-nu-u₂ gišBANŠUR KU₃.SI₂₂ a-na d60 u an-tu₄

o 18'18'

ša₂ ANe tu-kan-nu A-MEŠ ŠU-MIN a-na d60 u₃ an-tu₄ ša₂ ANe IL₂ši-ma

o 19'19'

gišBANŠUR ta-rak-kas₂ UZU GU₄ UZU UDU.NITA₂ u₃ MUŠEN-ḪI.A ta-rak-kas₂ KAŠ reš-tu-u₂

o 20'20'

a-di GEŠTIN SUR.RA tu-kan-nu GURUN gišKIRI₆ DU₃.A.BI tu-šar-ra-aḫ

o 21'21'

gišERIN.SIG₂ u₃ ZI₃.MAD.GA₂ ina muḫ-ḫi NIG₂.NAqa KU₃.SI₂₂ ta-sar-raq-ma

(o 21') You strew conifer needles and maṣhatu-flour on a gold censer and you pour (from) a gold libation-vessel (full) of the pressed wine. On 7 gold exalted offering tables you raise the water for the hands towards Jupiter, Venus, Mercury, Saturn and Mars, (and) the Moon and the Sun, as soon as they appear, and you set out the offering table.

o 22'22'

ma-aq-qu-u₂ KU₃.SI₂₂ GEŠTIN SUR.RA ta-naq-qa ina muḫ-ḫi 7 gišBANŠUR MAḪ KU₃.SI₂₂

o 23'23'

a-na dSAG.ME.GAR ddele-bat dGU₄.U₄ dGENNA u₃ dṣal-bat-a-nu d30

o 24'24'

u₃ dUTU ki-ma ša₂ in-nam-mar A-MEŠ ŠU-MIN IL₂ši-ma gišBANŠUR ta-rak-kas₂

o 25'25'

UZU GU₄ UZU UDU.NITA₂ u₃ MUŠEN-ḪI.A ta-rak-kas₂ KAŠ reš-tu-u₂ a-di GEŠTIN SUR.RA

(o 25') You set out meat from an ox, meat from a ram, and birds. You set up finest beer together with pressed wine. You make all the orchard fruit splendid (as offerings). You strew maṣhatu-flour and conifer needles on 7 gold censers and you pour (from) a gold libation-vessel (full) of the pressed wine. The lumahhu-priest who is wearing a girdle lights with fire of sulphur the great torch that is studded with aromatics, sprinkled with good oil and has completed the mouth-washing ritual, and he takes up position opposite the offering table and raises his hands for 'Great Anu of heaven' and speaks the invocation 'Anu, lord of the heavens'. The exalted offering table is cleared away and he raises the water for the hands.

o 26'26'

tu-kan-nu GURUN gišKIRI₆ DU₃.A.BI tu-šar-ra-aḫ ina muḫ-ḫi 7 NIG₂.NAqa KU₃.SI₂₂

o 27'27'

ZI₃.MAD.GA₂ u₃ lu-uk-šu₂ ta-sar-raq-ma ma-aq-qu-u₂ KU₃.SI₂₂ GEŠTIN SUR.RA

o 28'28'

ta-naq-qa LU₂.MAḪ ša₂ tug₂IB₂.LA₂ rak-su GI.IZI.LA₂ GALu₂ ša₂ ŠIM-ḪI.A

o 29'29'

su-un-nu-uš I₃.GIŠ DU₁₀.GA sal-ḫu u₃ KA.LUḪ.U₃.U₄.DA šu-pu-uš

o 30'30'

ina IZI PIŠ₁₀.dID₂ i-qa-dam-ma a-na tar-ṣi gišBANŠUR i-tar-ra-aṣ-ma

o 31'31'

ŠU-MIN-su a-na da-nu₃ GALu₂ ša₂ ANe IL₂ši-ma muld60 e-tel-lu ša₂-ma-mi

o 32'32'

naq-bit i-qab-bi gišBANŠUR MAḪ DU₈ar₂-ma A-MEŠ ŠU-MIN IL₂ši

o 33'33'

lu₂KU₄.E₂ GALu₂ ŠU-MIN GI.IZI.LA₂ ina lu₂MAŠ.MAŠ-MEŠ lu₂GALA-MEŠ u₃ lu₂NAR-MEŠ

(o 33') Among the mašmaššu-priests, the kalû-priests and the musicians, the chief temple-enterer takes the hands of the torch (leading) from the ziggurat, and he (the torch) goes into the main court (through) the Kasikila gate at the rear of the cella, and takes up position within Kizalaga (facing) towards Anu. A harû-offering is split open in front of him (Anu).

o 34'34'

TA ziq-qur-rat DABbat-am-ma KA₂.SIKIL.LA ša₂ ku-tal pa-pa-ḫa a-na KISAL.MAḪ KU₄-ma

Reverse
r 1r 1

it-ti KI.ZALAG₂.GA a-na d60 LA₂aṣ dugḫa-ru-u₂ ina IGI-šu₂ ib-bat-ta-qa

r 22

naq-bit i-qab-bi lu₂KU₄.E₂ ŠU-MIN GI.IZI.LA₂ dPAP.SUKKAL dNUSKA dARA

(r 2) He speaks an invocation. A temple-enterer takes the hands of the torch, Papsukkal, Nusku, Usmu and Messagunug and they go into the cella of Antu and take up position (facing) towards Antu. A harû-offering is split open in front of her. Papsukkal, Nusku, Usmu and Messagunug come out with the torch into the Ubšukkinnakku and beside the Dais of Destinies an ox is slaughtered in front of them. A pile of brushwood is lit in the Ubšukkinnakku from the torch. He takes away the shoulder of the ox together with its hide and he touches the right and left of the pile of brushwood. Papsukkal, Nusku, Usmu and Messagunug come out with the torch from the Ubšukkinnakku, (through) the Kamah gate, into the street, and they go and circle the temple, Mesagunug in front of him (and) Papsukkal, Nusku and Usmu with him. They (Papsukkal, Nusku and Usmu) come back, Papsukkal at the Kamah gate, Nusku at Kagal and Usmu at Kasag.

r 33

u₃ dMES.SAG.UNUGki DABbat-ma a-na e₂pa-pa-ḫa an-tu₄ DUak-MEŠ-ma

r 44

a-na an-tu₄ LA₂aṣ dugḫa-ru-u₂ ina pa-ni-šu₂ ib-bat-ta-qa dPAP.SUKKAL

r 55

dNUSKA dARA u₃ dMES.SAG.UNUGki it-ti GI.IZI.LA₂ ana UB.ŠU.UKKIN.NA.KI

r 66

E₃-MEŠ-nim-ma ina DA BARA₂—NAM-MEŠ GU₄ ina pa-ni-šu₂-nu im-maḫ-ḫa-aṣ IZI.ḪA.MUN

r 77

TA GI.IZI.LA₂ ina UB.ŠU.UKKIN.NA.KI in-nap-pa-aḫ uzuZA₃.LU GU₄ a-di KUŠ-šu₂

r 88

iṭ-ṭi-ir-ma 15 u 2.30 ša₂ ab-ri i-lap-pat dPAP.SUKKAL dNUSKA dARA

r 99

u₃ dMES.SAG.UNUGki it-ti GI.IZI.LA₂ TA UB.ŠU.UKKIN.NA.KI KA₂.MAḪ

r 1010

a-na su-u₂-qa E₃-MEŠ-nim-ma dMES.SAG.UNUGki ina pa-ni-šu₂ dPAP.SUKKAL dNUSKA

r 1111

u₃ dARA it-ti-šu₂ DUak-MEŠ-ma E₂ NIGINu₂ GURru-MEŠ-nim-ma

r 1212

dPAP.SUKKAL ina KA₂.MAḪ dNUSKA ina KA₂.GAL u₃ dARA ina KA₂.SAG

r 1313

lu₂KU₄.E₂ TA GI.IZI.LA₂ IZI.ḪA.MUN ina pa-ni-šu₂-nu u₂-ša₂-aṣ-ba-at-ma

(r 13) The temple-enterer kindles a brushwood pile in front of them from the torch and sits until daylight. The šangû-priests of the temples of the gods of Tirana (and) similarly a temple-enterer of all the gods light a lamp from the torch and carry (it) to the temples of their gods and perform the well-being of the temple (ceremony). They light a brushwood pile at the gate of the temples of their gods.

r 1414

a-di ZALAG₂ TUŠab lu₂SANGA-MEŠ ša₂ E₂-MEŠ DINGIR-MEŠ TIR.AN.NAki ša₂-niš lu₂KU₄.E₂ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI

r 1515

nu-ur₂ TA GI.IZI.LA₂ i-qad₂-du-u₂-ma a-na E₂ DINGIR-MEŠ-šu₂-nu IL₂ši-ma

r 1616

ša₂-lam E₂ ip-pu-uš IZI.ḪA.MUN ina KA₂ E₂ DINGIR-MEŠ-šu₂-nu i-nap!(ERIM-)pa-aḫ

r 1717

d60 uš-ta-pa-a ina nap-ḫar KUR.KUR u₃ it-ta-ṣa-a ṣa-lam ba-nu-u₂ naq-bit i-qab-bu-u₂

(r 17) They speak the invocations 'Anu is made visible in all the lands' and 'He has come forth, the beautiful constellation'.

r 1818

GI.IZI.LA₂ u₃ dMES.SAG.UNUGki GURru-nim-ma a-na KISAL an-tu₄ KU₄-ma

(r 18) The torch and Messagunug come back and go into the forecourt of Antu and take up position (facing) towards Antu. A mašmaššu-priest extinguishes the torch with water from the holy water vessel, finest beer, milk, wine and oil. Messagunug goes and sits in the Ubšukkinnakku until dawn. Adad, Sin, Šamaš and Belet-ili sit in the forecourt until dawn. The people of the land light a brushwood pile in their houses.

r 1919

a-na an-tu₄ LA₂aṣ lu₂MAŠ.MAŠ ina A-MEŠ dugA.GUB₂.BA KAŠ SAG GA GEŠTIN u I₃.GIŠ

r 2020

GI.IZI.LA₂ u₂-na-<<an>>-aḫ dMES.SAG.UNUGki DUak-ma ina UB.ŠU.UKKIN.NA.KI

r 2121

a-di na-ma-ri TUŠab dIŠKUR d30 dUTU u₃ dbe-let—DINGIR-MEŠ ina KISAL

r 2222

a-di na-ma-ri TUŠab UN-MEŠ KUR ina E₂-MEŠ-šu₂-nu IZI.ḪA.MUN i-nap-pa-aḫ

r 2323

SISKUR₂ qe₂-re-e-ti a-na d60 an-tu₄ u₃ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI i-naq-qu-u₂

(r 23) They present an offering of a banquet to Anu, Antu and all the gods.

r 2424

naq-bit ki-ma maḫ-ri-im-ma i-qab-bu-u₂ lu₂EN.NUN IRI ina su-u₂-qa-MEŠ

(r 24) They speak the invocations as before. A city guard lights a brushwood pile in the streets and squares. He does not hold back the city gates of Uruk until dawn. The guard of the city gates erects standards to the right and left of the city gates. He lights brushwood piles at the city gates at dawn.

r 2525

u₃ SILA.LIM₂.MA IZI.ḪA.MUN i-nap-pa-aḫ KA₂.GAL-MEŠ UNUGki a-di na-ma-ri

r 2626

ul uḫ-ḫe-er lu₂EN.NUN KA₂.GAL-MEŠ giURI₃.GAL-MEŠ 15 u₃ 2.30

r 2727

ša₂ KA₂.GAL-MEŠ u₂-zaq-qa-pu ab!(NAB-)ri a-na na-ma-ri ina KA₂.GAL-MEŠ i-nap-pa-⸢aḫ⸣

r 2828

U₄ 17-KAM 10 UŠ u₄-mu EGIR KURḫa dUTU KA₂ ina pa-ni d60 u an-tu₄ BADte-ma ba-a-a-⸢at⸣

(r 28) The 17th day, 10 UŠ (into) the day, after sunrise the gate in front of Anu and Antu is opened and the nocturnal offering is cleared away. The main meal of the morning is presented to Anu, Antu and all the gods.

r 2929

DU₈ar₂ nap-tan GALu₂ ša₂ še-ri₃ a-na d60 an-tu₄ u₃ DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI i-qar-ru-⸢ub⸣

r 3030

GALu₂ DU₈-ma tar-den-nu i-qar-ru-ub NIG₂.NAqa-MEŠ KU₃.SI₂₂ u₂-mal₂-le-e-ma ⸢SISKUR₂⸣

(r 30) The main meal is cleared away and the secondary meal is presented. He fills the gold censers and presents an offering, an ox and a ram. The musicians sing. [...] the invocation 'Honoured one, wild bull'.

r 3131

GU₄ u₃ UDU.NITA₂ BALqi₂ lu₂NAR-MEŠ i-za-am-mu-ru e-lum gudsumun₂-na ⸢naq?⸣-[bit? ...]

r 3232

ina IGI DINGIR-MEŠ DU₃.A.BI NIG₂.NAqa-MEŠ u₂-mal₂-le-e-ma SISKUR₂ GU₄ u₃ UDU.NITA₂ ⸢BAL⸣[qi₂]

(r 32) He fills censers in front of all the gods and presents an offering, an ox and a ram.

r 3333

ŠU-MIN dIŠKUR d30 d⸢UTU⸣ dMES.SAG.UNUGki u₃ dbe-let—DINGIR-MEŠ ina šid-di ⸢GADA⸣ [...]

(r 33) [...] the hands of Adad, Sin, Šamaš, Messagunug and Belet-ili in the curtains of linen

r 3434

x ⸢MEŠ?⸣ x x [...] x x [...]

(r 34) ... [...] ... [...]
